body_txt: The villain has kicked their fair share of puppies , laid waste to cities, farms, and fields, killed the heroes' loved ones, and generally been a downright Jerkass to everyone. They've doublecrossed The Dragon , and burnt bridges. Now the heroes are knocking at, or more specifically, down, the doors of their sanctum , and the villain has realized that this is likely not going to end well for them if they're caught, and so they decide that running is a perfectly valid option. It is, after all, what they're known for. Except that their escape route has been cut off. Maybe The Dragon decided that they want to turn the tables and be the boss themselves , or they've had a Heel–Face Turn . Maybe the heroes anticipated the villain's throne room having an escape pod and they disabled it. Whatever the case may be, there's nowhere left for them to run, and justice is coming for them. What happens next depends on whether they decide to surrender, pretend to surrender , or at least Face Death with Dignity and fight to the last. Just as a Genre Savvy hero will deny a villain their exit, so too will a savvy villain try to make it look like they died in the encounter , though this leaves the problem of Never Found the Body (and might, in more extreme circumstances, cost the villain An Arm and a Leg , or see their face horribly disfigured ). Compare Exit, Pursued by a Bear , wherein a villain tries to escape, only to get chased away by a hungry and/or angry animal. See also Anti-Escape Mechanism and Falling into Jail , for when the escape attempt leads the villain to jail. Compare Anti-Interference Lock Up , which is often the villains confining their victims. See Hoist by His Own Petard , if the villain ends up confined where they have kept somebody else prisoner. As the capture/death of the Big Bad is often an ending trope, and, in some instances, might even reveal who the Big Bad is, beware of unmarked spoilers .
